speaker: interviewer
text: --seven. This is an interview with Bernhard Storch. In June, you were still in the army but you were able to go visit your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: In June. Yes. Before-- the-- the beginning of June. It was probably the 2nd or the 3rd of June, I was able to get a pass. My commanding officer promised me a pass. While still in Germany, I approached him about it. And he promised me he would give me a pass. And of course, I was the first one to receive a pass. And I went to see my hometown all by myself in uniform, and with my gun and whatever. And I came to Bochnia. I came from-- from-- from Siedlce to Warsaw, from Warsaw to Bochnia, to Krakow, from Krakow to Bochnia. And we-- we had-- I came to-- to the railroad station. And from the railroad station, I simply walk home because it was something like 3-- 3 and 3/4-- 3/4 kilometers away from the-- from the train station. So it was a easy walk. And I came home. And the first one which I approached is my next-door neighbor. And sure enough, she was home. And she did not recognize me because I was in uniforms. She knew that I was in the army, but-- not that I was in the army. She knew that I was in the Soviet Union, in the camp. But she didn't know that I was in the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she almost fainted when she saw me. And we started-- both started to cry, and this and that. After a few minutes, everything settled down. And we went to business. I saw that my house is not standing. And there's a garden there. So I ask her, how did it happen? She said, the Germans, when they came after the ghetto was liquidating-- when the ghetto was liquidating, they came and they simply tore the house down. And there's a garden. People are planting there. Probably herself too. Or the next door neighbor. Is she the one who told you what happened to your mother and brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: She is the one which told me that they went to ghetto, and that the ghetto was liquidated in 1942. She did not tell me exactly that-- how or when or what. She just told me that the ghetto was liquidated and ghetto. She didn't know-- she says, people were sent-- they were sent to Belzec, and they were sent to Auschwitz. But she does not know which way she-- which way who went. Now-- so I had a short visit. I did not stay over in her house. I was looking for people which-- Jewish people which came back. I-- somebody told me-- as a matter of fact, she told me that down the street, there's a family, two brothers which survive in Hungary. And they came back to Bochnia. And they are there. And then there's a-- people congregate at one of the places by Schanser. Schanser was a manufacturer of soap. And Schanser is the one which donated the synagogue to the city, and the synagogue was named Schanser Synagogue. And that's the synagogue which I was bar-- bar mitzvah in. And so I went to that build-- to that house. And I met one of the Schanser kid only survived. Aaron Schanser was his name. Later I found out he passed away in Israel. But Aaron Schanser survived. And then there were eight other people there. I don't know everybody's name. But one of the girls was-- her name was Schneider. Schneider [? Ruvna. ?] And she was a colleague of mine. We belonged to the same Zionist organization. There were a couple of other people there. And of course, the question came up how and who and when and where. So they said-- they mentioned to me that part of the people went to Belzec and some-- and part of it went to Auschwitz. And that my three brothers and my cousin, which she knew too-- they played together, they went to school together. She was younger than I am. They went with the same transport to Auschwitz. Now, you know Auschwitz-- you go to the left and you go to the right. Unfortunately, they went to the different direction. They were sent in a different direction. Those people were sent to the right direction for them, so they survived. So I know for a fact that my three brothers and that cousin of mine went to Auschwitz. I also was told that those three brothers, on their way to Belzec-- they were originally sent once to Belzec-- ran away from the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were shrewd little kids. They were able to jump out from the train. How, I don't know. I was told by that. They returned back. They returned back to Bochnia. And of course, where do they go? They go again to the ghetto. And for the second time on the second return, they were taken to Auschwitz when they were liquidating the ghetto. And you never heard where your mother was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never heard with my mother. My mother, or Belzec or Auschwitz. I know where my grandparents died. My grandparents died-- they had a special place for them-- which if I say for them, for all the people. About seven kilometers away from Bochnia, there was a small town, village town, a farm town, of [POLISH]. And they had tremendous big forests there. And the German decided that that's going to be a grave for the senior citizen there. And that's where they died. That's where they died. And couple-- couple thousands of them died. And also in between-- there is a big-- I haven't gone there. I didn't go there. But I also-- as I mentioned to you in private conversation, I met somebody which which-- which is from Bochnia, and he told me exactly what happened because he lived through. Eventually he wind up himself in Auschwitz. He was a Pole. But he said there is a-- he was there, and there's a plaque erected in Polish and in Hebrew. And some-- I don't know if they have names or not. I think there are names listed there of the people which had died. And it's sort of like a monument for the people which died. There's about 500 Gentile people, and couple of thousand people of-- Jewish people. 1,500 Jewish people from the surrounded area. Not only from Bochnia. Because Bochnia had only 2 and 1/2 thousand. But from the whole surrounding area was something like, I don't know, 14,000 or 15,000 Jews altogether.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now how long did you stay in Bochnia?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bochnia I stayed six days. About so. I stayed six or five days. I stayed two days at the-- at the mayor's house, because I went to him to thank him. For this thing, he invited me to his house. And we had, you know, quite a discussion on the whole thing. He was a Pole. And he came to the-- to the prominence after the war because he was with the partisans. So he got that job as a mayor of the town. And I slept one night with the Farber brothers close to my home. And the rest, I slept two days with that thing. And the rest I slept with the Schanser family. Family. Just know that one--

speaker: interviewer
text: One.

speaker: interviewee
text: --one son. And there were other-- eight other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you were released from the army in September.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was released from the army in September.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went-- I went to Katowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you arranged to go there--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that your future wife would come there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I did not arrange to go there. They arranged. When they were-- they were-- because I was in the army, they had the preference to go out from this town first. Families. She was listed-- she was listed-- mind you, she was-- I wasn't even engaged. She was listed as a wife. And of course of her sisters and everything. One of her sisters, her husband was in the-- in the army. So it was easy to get them out. So she came-- they came, actually, with the first transport from the Soviet Union. And they went to the towns which we know, because my uncle also lived in [POLISH], which is 10 kilometers away.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they went to Katowice, because that was the-- that was the state capital. Beautiful city. And it was larger than [POLISH]. So that-- that's where they wanted to-- they didn't want to go to [POLISH] anymore. So they went to Katowice. And of course, that's where I got discharged. In Katowice, I asked to be discharged in Katowice. But it-- it was a whole combination there. I mean, you know, I had papers to go to Soviet Union, and to get the families down.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I knew that they going to be here anyway, and I didn't want to go to Soviet Union-- I didn't want to go-- I didn't even want to see Soviet Union anymore. So I stayed. And I know-- knew about it because I was in touch with my future wife. And I knew about it that they have already papers to come. So-- and we were discharged in September. They were-- they came-- I think they came in August.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married 18 of-- of November, 1945, in Katowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in Katowice after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in Katowice till spring of 1946. And then, of course, illegally we left Poland to Germany. Irony, no? To Germany. But that was the only way out, to Germany. And then we registered to Palestine or to the United States. We registered both. On both items. Whoever which had come first, we would go. Fortunately for us, United States came first. And that's what we got. And my-- my--

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you get here?

speaker: interviewee
text: --my aunt was very mad at me because we didn't come to Palestine. [LAUGHS] She was living there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had an uncle here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had an uncle here, so-- so we had to split somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what day did you arrive here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we got here-- we arrived here in New York City. I remember the day. The 22nd of April, 1947. So it took us-- didn't take us long. Because about an hour and-- I mean, an hour. Took us about a year and a half to get from that point, or a little bit less than that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And here in the United States, you worked in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In United--

speaker: interviewer
text: --tailoring and women's clothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had a shop of your own.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, let me-- let me say that I have my-- as I mentioned, my uncle. And we found out about my uncle already that he's going to pick me up just when we were already on the debarkation point. Because HIAS, as I said, arranged for him to get us. And they found him for me. They found him for me. And he picked us up, and we stayed with my uncle for three weeks. And he had a small apartment, three bedroom apartment, three kids. Not a house. In a private home. In a two family home. Two or three family home in Italian section of Brooklyn, East 2nd Street right off Washington Parkway. And he picked us up. And he farmed out those two boys to their friends. One of the boys was like three years younger than I am. And the other one was three years younger than the older brother. And then there was a girl, which was a teenager. He was-- she was about 15 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she stayed home, and those two boys were sleeping with their friends. And they did give us their own bedroom. So we occupied their own bedroom. And they slept in the-- one of the other rooms. And we stayed with them, and they adopt us just like children.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in the beginning, it was a little bit-- you know, it was not so-- not so [YIDDISH], because he explained me later. He says, don't mind if I ask you all those question because a lot of people are coming to the States which they claim that they have relatives, and unfortunately they turn out later that they didn't have any relatives. That they were not relatives. They just knew names, and that's why they came. So don't mind if I ask you all those question. We spoke at that time German because he was served in the German-- I mean, in the Austrian army. So he was fluent in German. And if we forgot something, it's so-- you know, it's [? shorten ?] in Yiddish. My tante-- my-- my tante. My aunt did not speak because she came from Odessa. So she speak Russian and Yiddish. That's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in any event-- so I-- you know, I-- we had a nice conversation with this thing. He says, I'm your son. [LAUGHS] I'm your real nephew. Not a-- Not a fake nephew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now a fake nephew. And of course, you know, on-- I mentioned the date when Father passed away. He knew about it. And I knew the Jewish calendar, so that even was more stronger than that. And the whole family, he knew the whole family. My mother's family. So it was OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, we should just mention that you had two children here, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had two children. We married in November. My daughter was born in 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also November, in the United States. And she was born November 9. My birthday is November 10. So we always celebrate together. And my son was born on April 4, 1954, also in New York City. And they both married. My daughter's divorced, but she has a teenage kid. Not a teenage kid. He's-- my oldest grandson is 25. He was 25 in August. And she got divorced when-- when they were married six years, unfortunately. She never remarried. And my grandson is adopted child. He was adopted by my son-in-law and my-- by my daughter when he was a year and a half old. He came to the States when he was three years old. That was the different in time. From Vietnam. They were adopt from Vietnam, as a Vietnamese child. He's-- he's being-- I mean, he was bar mitzvah. He's a full-fledged Jew. And he believes in the religion. And of course, he wouldn't desert it. [LAUGHS] So it-- and as a matter of fact, my rabbat-- my Rabbi Sosland, which is here in the city, he performed a ceremony, the conversion. And his bar mitzvah. Everything was done here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have two other grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two other grandchildren from my son. And they are 10. Joshua is 10. And Rebecca is seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you mentioned to me that it wasn't until, what, about 10 or 15 years ago that you began talking about your experiences during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: 25 years after. 25.

speaker: interviewer
text: You waited 25 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did it take so long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SIGHS], first of all it was too painful. You didn't have too many listeners to listen to. I opened up in the beginning, you know, in my shop. You know, where I started to work and everything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: People looked at you like crazy. And the question came out. Listen, everybody there. How come you are here? OK. So many people are coming in. You know, and those are Jewish people we're talking about here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was just too painful to answer all those question. I just didn't do it. My uncle knew exactly that I was in the army. He knew exactly what happened during the war. And even so, you know, it was still-- he knew the slaughters, because he was in First World War. But he didn't know something like that really happens.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had hard time to convince the people. So we just didn't talk about it. You know, we talk about every-- everything except that. Everything about that. And then finally, it-- slowly people started to talk, and this and that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was bottle it up, really. I was sick to my stomach because I knew there is something to get out or I get crazy. And I almost did. So finally, you know, after 25 years, I just started to talk about it, slowly. Now, my kids knew it. My kids knew it. My daughter knew it when she was eight years-- even before that. She knew it precise. She did not know precisely what happened. And the reason for it is, you know, children have birthdays here in the United States. Children have parents. Children have grandparents. And children have another grandparent. There are always two grand-- two things. She came once from one of my close friend. They were here in the United States. They came from Germany. German Jews, as a matter of fact. We belong to the same synagogue in Washington Heights.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the kids were friendly. They were going to the kindergarten and everything. And she was about six years old. And she said, Daddy. How come I don't have any grandparents? Everybody has grandparents. So I said there was a long-- I didn't said it precisely about concentration camp, because it was-- it was still too early. I said, there was a long war in Europe, and your parents and your uncles and your great grandparents died in that war.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the way-- by the time they were eight years old, they knew everything what was going on, both of them. So that was the end of it. I had to-- I couldn't keep any secret anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because once a child ask you, how come? You have to answer. You have to answer. Straight answer. Without hesitation. Otherwise they wouldn't believe you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's exactly what we did. So they knew it. Well, it happened right after her birthday party when all those grandparents were there, and there were no grandparents here. So that's-- that's when it started, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you've been interviewed for the Jewish Military History Museum in Washington, DC.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh-- by the way, yes. I joined the Jewish War Veterans here. I joined the Jewish War Veterans in the city Post 756. I'm a live member right now from the Jewish War Veterans. I was twice commended with the Post 756 in the city. Presently, I'm the district commander of district council of the Jewish War Veterans here in Rockland County. And I represent eight different posts under my command, including Congressman Gilman. He belongs to one of my posts. [LAUGHS] So I take orders from him. He takes orders from me. And I'm very proud of the Jewish War Veterans, of the history of them, because they try to-- they try to preserve the legacy, really, of the Jewish fighting people. And there were the Jewish-- what the Jewish War Veterans have here in the United States, none of other veteran organization has it. Which they have a museum. And by the way, the Jewish War Veterans are celebrating now, this year-- this year. It started in March. 100 anniversary of the Jewish War Veterans here in the United States, which makes in the oldest organization, fighting organization, in the United States. Next comes the American Legion, which is 25 years younger.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm very proud of that, that they try to preserve the legacy. And for that reason, they have a museum since 1957 in Washington-- Washington, DC. And it was enacted by Congress. Not too many people know that. It was enacted by Congress in 1957, opened up in 1958 officially. And it's located 1811 R Street.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's where the military history is there. And being-- they have-- they open up the exhibit in 1944, and the theme of the exhibit was liberating concentration camps. And that's how they called me. They knew the history. Because you submit the history. They called me, and if I have any artifacts from the war, if I would be willing to-- to lend to them for the time when they have the exhibit there. I said, of course it would be. I didn't have a uniform because they didn't let you out with uniforms in Europe. So I-- I made a duplicate uniform, the way it was done there. I made a duplicate uniform. And that uniform is on display there. And there is some medals there. There are still three medals. The big medal which is here, I didn't have it on that time. And because I retrieve-- as I mentioned to you, this medal was retrieved in 1994 through the Polish Council here in New York City. In any event, three medals are there. There is a taped history, short history, which you-- when you pick up the receiver, it's-- you know, I-- my voice comes through there. And it will be there for the past three years. And also, the same exhibit, miniature exhibit was there. Not the uniforms as-- until-- just miniature and picture exhibit. Was displayed at the Intrepid Museum in New York City from March, 1995-- March, 1995 till September, 1995. And it was a great success there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were interviewed by the BBC--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- Yeah. I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --for a program on liberators.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was liberate-- yes. I was interviewed by the BBC on the-- in 1994. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the exhibit-- I mean, the-- the show opened up in Britain in January. And it opened up throughout Europe, including Israel. That-- it was broadcasting by the BBC. In Britain, it was broadcast on Channel 4. And it was a very lengthy interview, the way we doing now. Except there is only about three or four minutes there because there is about 16 or 18 different soldiers. Fortunately, I was only the only one from the Polish Army. There is two-- were two Russian generals which are also interviewed, and they occupied Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the description there, the show opens up, actually, with my segment of liberating Majdanek, because that was the first ever extermination or concentration camp liberated. Was the first ever, was Majdanek. Of course, Sobibor didn't count because there was nothing there. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's the reason they opened up that show.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm very proud of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Jewish War Veterans have also a tape, which eventually I will show it to you. It was a short tape, half an hour tape. Also a very, very broadly. And it's on display-- as a matter of fact, that tape is on display by the producer of that tape in Washington, DC, in-- in the museum, in the Holocaust Museum in Washington, DC. And a catalog is with it also, which also is on sale for the profit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you also speak to schoolchildren. You've spoken at West Point.

speaker: interviewee
text: I definitely-- yes. I speak to-- to children, high school levels. And I also addressed a symposium also with the-- with the high school teachers. It was done at Ramsey College one year. We had 40 teachers assembled. And there were four speakers altogether. And it went very, very well, true. And yes, I do. Whoever ask me through the Holocaust Center in Spring Valley. And if somebody ask me privately, I do it. I do it just to inform people. Not one single penny is taken in, or something like that. Yes. I do that. I was invited. I'm very proud to say that I was invited by the class of 1995 just before the graduation at West Point. I'm involved in West Point, so they know me there. They know partially my history, because I don't brag about it. But they know it. And I'm invite-- I'm involved in-- in the Jewish chapel there. I'm co-chairman of the Jewish activities at West Point. As you know, we have a beautiful chapel there. And there's now about 60 Jewish cadets, girls and boys. And so I'm invited to address the cadets in 1995 just before the graduation in April, '95, by the rabbi there. And it went very, very well too. It was about an hour and a half lecture which went very well through. And I was invited also by them privately and before when they have more time to come and to be in their living room, in their quarters, and already talk soldier to soldier. So I would take them up on that. We will do it. But that strictly was talk about liberation concentration camps. And that thing. And I-- that's the only thing which I do, what I saw. I cannot say that I was in the camp, because I wasn't. Even so, I, you know, lost the whole family. But nevertheless, it is not very pleasant. And it's very painful. Believe it not, after so many years, it's very painful to talk. I get tremendous headaches after that. But it's worth it. To inform people, it's worth it. And I really don't know how long we have on this earth. And as long as we can continue, lot of people would show the same thing which I did unfortunately didn't make it through the war. So we have to speak for them. And as far as the medals from the Polish government. Yes, I received medals. Some in Poland. But mostly, I left everything there. I just take the documentation which I have. I took with me. I was afraid to transport medals from Poland to Germany, first of all. And if my wife would have known that I took the documents, she would have killed me on the spot because, you know, it-- it was not smart. But I couldn't part with it. So after the Polish regime, the communist regime, fell there, I started to write a letter to the consulate here in New York City. And documented. I have this and this and that. And I would like to have them and get back. And it started in 1991. And I just handwrit-- you know, hand write a letter to him. Did-- didn't take it for-- just took it for granted, you know. He would not even look at it. And I sent it to him where I was born and everything else, what I did, and this and that. And went through about a week time. I get a real form to fill out, and my father's name, my mother's name, and everything else. And give him everything in detail. And sure enough, I got all my medals which I received during the war here. And it lasted from 1992 to 1994. The last one was in 1994. So I got everything back through that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you wanted to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: What else could I add?

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we've covered most things.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we covered everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's obvious that you feel it's very important to tell your story--

speaker: interviewee
text: It is. It is.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and you have told it.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's absolutely very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we appreciate it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I urge everybody, no matter how hard it is, we have to tell it. There are not too many of us left, and whoever is left is over 70 years old. And you know, thank God I'm still can do it. Even if I had a bypass already. But I still can do it. And as long as-- as my mind is here, this mouth will talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we appreciate your telling us--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: --this story. It's very important.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. It was my pleasure. As a matter of fact, it was my pleasure. As I was approached by the Spielberg Foundation through the Jewish War Veterans. And it was my pleasure to be here with you, and I thank you all of them. All of you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And we'll do the photographs--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we do the photographs.

speaker: interviewer
text: --on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
